coredata.dc:title: Experimental and simulative insight into graphite-coated Ti substrate with high physicochemical stability
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Wensheng
Gao, Yue
Xie, Quan
Gao, Tinghong
Liang, Yongchao
Chen, Qian
Tian, Zean
Ruan, Yunjun
coredata.dc:description: The Ti substrate, with physicochemical stability and excellent electrical conductivity, is potential to fabricate all-solid-state batteries. However, the Titanium atoms at high-temperature synthesis makes it easy to react with active materials. Modifying the titanium surface with an inert protective layer while ensuring high electrical conductivity remains a significant challenge. Herein, a pencil was used to draw on the Ti substrate and subsequently sintering at a high temperature to obtain a graphite-coated Ti substrate (Ti-Graphite). The experimental results show that Ti-Graphite has a smoother surface and fewer defects in the graphite layer. Moreover, titanium carbide is formed at the interface of graphite and Ti substrate, which will enhance the physicochemical stability of the Ti-Graphite. To gain deep understanding of the evolution mechanism in the synthesis, molecular dynamics simulations were carried out to investigate it. The results exhibit that the voids at the C/Ti interface and stress in the model were significantly decreased after sintering and cooling treatment, proving that Ti-Graphite has a smoother surface, a more stable interface, and fewer defects at the atomic scale. Therefore, we provide a simple and facile strategy to obtain a Ti-graphite substrate with superior physicochemical stability, which reaveals excellent potential to synthesize high-performance all-solid-state batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphite-coating
Titanium substrate
Molecular dynamics simulations
Sintering mechanism

ZK[2021] 051, and [2017] 5788), the Industry and Education Combination Innovation Platform of Intelligent Manufacturing and Graduate Joint Training Base at Guizhou University (Grant No: 2020-520000-83-01-324061 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 52262021 , and 51761004 ), the Scientific and Technological Plan Project of Guizhou Province (Grant No. [2021]060), and the Guizhou Province Graduate Research Fund YJSCXJH[2020]037.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of graphite-coated Ti substrate.
Morphological characterization: side view of (a) Ti/Graphite and (b) Ti-Graphite with EDS, top view of (c) Ti/Graphite and (d) Ti-Graphite.
3D map test results of the step meter by (a) Ti/Graphite and (b) Ti-Graphite.
The atom force microscope of (a) Ti/Graphite and (b) Ti-Graphite.
Structural and electrochemical characterization of Ti/Graphite and Ti-Graphite: (a) Raman spectra, (b) XRD patterns.
Model for molecular dynamics simulation calculations. The gray, dark green and red spheres represent Ti atoms. The blue spheres are graphite.
Three snapshots of graphite-coated titanium on different processes: (a) completed coating, (b) sintering at 1026 K, and (c) cooling to 293 K.
Surface morphology of the models for the three simulated processes: (a) completed coating, (b) sintering at 1026 K, and (c) cooling to 293 K.
Stress distribution of the model at (a) 1026 K and (b) 293 K.
The average energy distribution of the carbon and titanium atoms under different temperatures.
Distribution of defects and their dislocation line types in the model after cooled to 293 K.
RMS roughness of the substrate at three moments.

head.ce:title: Experimental and simulative insight into graphite-coated Ti substrate with high physicochemical stability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Optoelectronic Materials and Technology, College of Big Data and Information Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Optoelectronic Materials and Technology
College of Big Data and Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Optoelectronic Materials and Technology, College of Big Data and Information Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Ti substrate, with physicochemical stability and excellent electrical conductivity, is potential to fabricate all-solid-state batteries. However, the Titanium atoms at high-temperature synthesis makes it easy to react with active materials. Modifying the titanium surface with an inert protective layer while ensuring high electrical conductivity remains a significant challenge. Herein, a pencil was used to draw on the Ti substrate and subsequently sintering at a high temperature to obtain a graphite-coated Ti substrate (Ti-Graphite). The experimental results show that Ti-Graphite has a smoother surface and fewer defects in the graphite layer. Moreover, titanium carbide is formed at the interface of graphite and Ti substrate, which will enhance the physicochemical stability of the Ti-Graphite. To gain deep understanding of the evolution mechanism in the synthesis, molecular dynamics simulations were carried out to investigate it. The results exhibit that the voids at the C/Ti interface and stress in the model were significantly decreased after sintering and cooling treatment, proving that Ti-Graphite has a smoother surface, a more stable interface, and fewer defects at the atomic scale. Therefore, we provide a simple and facile strategy to obtain a Ti-graphite substrate with superior physicochemical stability, which reaveals excellent potential to synthesize high-performance all-solid-state batteries.

Through the investigation of experiments and molecular dynamics simulations, it was established that graphite coating can effectively enhance the properties of Ti.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium metal has a wide range of applications in aerospace, automotive manufacturing, and biomedical applications due to its lightweight, high strength, and excellent biocompatibility properties [1–4]. In addition, titanium and titanium alloys have recently received much attention in energy storage devices, often as collectors [5–9]. With the development of all-solid-state batteries, more and more studies are conducted to in-situ grow the active material on the collector [10], which inevitably undergoes high-temperature treatment. Although Ti is stable at room temperature, it is easily oxidized to titanium dioxide at high temperatures. The low conductivity of titanium dioxide limits the application of titanium in all-solid-state energy storage devices [11,12].

Graphite is an isomer of carbon, which is chemically stable and corrosion-resistant [13]. In the two-dimensional layer plane, the carbon atom bonds with three other carbon atoms through sp 2 hybridization to form a six-membered hexagonal ring [14]. The carbon atoms in the same layer leave one p z orbital, overlapping to form π bonds and delocalized electrons. These electrons can move freely inner the layer, so graphite has good chemical stability and metallic conductivity. Owing to its excellent properties, graphite has been regarded as a potential coating layer to improve the stability of metal and alloys [15,16]. Chemical and physical vapor deposition are widely utilized to prepare films with high purity and dense morphology [17,18]. Nevertheless, these technologies require complex conditions such as flat substrate surface, high pressure, high temperature, and expensive reaction devices, which will increase the complexity and cost of the operation [19–22]. In addition, uncontrollable nanopores can easily affect the performance of the coating. On the other hand, thermal reactive diffusion is considered as a promising alternative method for vapor phase deposition [23,24]. Therefore, exploring a simple and moderate carbon-coating technology with excellent quality remains challenging.

Carbon coating by pencil drawing and subsequent high-temperature annealing has attracted much attention due to the simplicity and ease of implementation of the preparation process. Li et al. [25] designed a durable zinc anode using graphite as a functional interface using a pencil coating. Subramanyan et al. [26] investigated the performance of half-cell and full-cell configurations using 6H pencil graphite as an anode for sodium-ion batteries, improving the long-cycle stability of the batteries. Trnkova et al. [27] modified pencil graphite with chemical and thermal treatments and investigated the morphological changes and changes in the electrochemical properties of pencil graphite. Chen et al. [28] reported that pencil graphite exfoliated into graphene with the help of nanoclay, which can be used as an energy storage electrode or current collector. Although some papers have reported carbon coating by a pencil, the interaction mechanism between the carbon layer and substrate has yet to be disclosed.

Molecular dynamics (MD) can simulate the interaction and evolution processes of molecular and atomic systems, playing a vital role in revealing the microscopic reaction mechanism. Alexandre et al. [29] used MD to construct a graphene/titanium interface to investigate graphene defects and the structure and energy of the graphene-Ti interface. Chen et al. [30] used first-principles calculations to systematically study the separation work and interface energy of Ti/graphene, Ti/CVG (Vacancy defective graphene) and Ti/graphite interfaces. Ou et al. [31] systematically demonstrated the thermal boundary conductivity of the Ti/graphene interface by MD simulations and proved its potential for cooling applications. Wang et al. [32] utilized MD to construct Cu, Ni, and Ti metal-coated reinforced graphene separately and embedded in an aluminum matrix to enhance the interfacial strength of the composites. At present, the evolution and mechanism of graphite coating on Ti substrate is difficult to be observed experimentally. To solve this issue, MD should be a suitable strategy to disclose the high-temperature resistance mechanism of pencil-coated Ti sheets.

This study investigated the graphite-coating, sintering, and cooling processes of pencil-drawn Ti substrate by experimental characterization and MD simulations. It was found that the protection of the graphite coating significantly improved the stability of the Ti substrate after sintering. Moreover, molecular dynamics simulations were conducted to probe the effect of graphite coating on the interfacial stress and the defect distribution in the sample.
All the reagents were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. and used without further purification.

Synthesis of Materials. Prepared a square titanium substrate with an area of 1 square centimeter and a thickness of 0.5 mm. Used a commercial pencil (2B pencil, M&G Chenguang Stationery Co., Lte.) to write back and forth on the titanium surface until the entire surface was completely covered by graphite. The sample surface is then rinsed with anhydrous ethanol and dried. Then, it was heated at 750 °C (1026K) for 1 h with a ramp rate of 5 °C min−1 under an argon atmosphere.

Structural Characterization. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained using a PANalytical B.V. Empyrean diffractometer. The morphologies and microstructures of the samples were observed by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, HITACHI, SU8010) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon). Molecular information of the samples was obtained by Raman spectrometry (Raman, Horiba, LabRAM HR Evolution). Thickness of the film comes from the Bruker DektakXT step profiler.

Electrochemical Measurement. A three-electrode cell was configured by the as-prepared graphite-coated Ti substrate working electrode, a platinum counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode in 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte, which was tested on a CHI760E electrochemical workstation.

All simulation processes are performed by the Large-scale Atomic/Molecular Massively Parallel Simulator (LAMMPS) package [33]. The whole simulation process is divided into three parts. Firstly, the model of graphite-coated Ti surface is simulated. Secondly, the relaxed model is rapidly heated up (sintered) at 1026 K. Thirdly, the model is rapidly cooling down to room temperature (293 K). Based on the experimental results, α-Ti with lattice constants of a = 2.956 Å and c = 4.691 Å [34] is used as the substrate in the simulation model. The velocity of the pencil scratch was set to 0.1 m s−1, and the coating behavior at room temperature with the simulated boundary condition of fpf (p is periodic, and f is non-periodic and fixed). The boundary condition of the second process is changed to pps with a shrinkable free boundary in the z-axis direction. The third step of the cooling process is the boundary condition of pps (s is non-periodic and shrink-wrapped).

The choice of the potential used to describe interatomic interactions can directly affect the results of the simulation calculations [35]. In this investigation, three types of potentials were used to describe the interactions between the different elements. Since the main component of a pencil is graphite, we use a model of graphite instead of a pencil, using an adaptive intermolecular reaction empirical bond order (AIREBO) potential to describe carbon-carbon bond interactions [36,37]. The interaction potential function between Ti atoms is based on the embedded atomic potential model (EAM-FS) theory given by Finnis and Sinclair [38]. An improved EAM-FS [39] is used to control the interaction potential within the Ti substrate. In addition, the interaction between the Ti metal matrix and graphite is described with a 12–6 Lennard-Jones (L-J) potential [40]. The selection of parameters for all potential functions is written in detail in the supplementary information file.

The process of graphite coating on the surface of the titanium substrate is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The titanium substrate is a square with a size of 1 cm2 and a thickness of 0.5 mm. A pencil is used to evenly coat a layer of graphite on the surface of the substrate (defined as Ti/Graphite). The sample is sintered under a protective gas atmosphere and then cooled down to room temperature (defined as Ti-Graphite).

Fig. 2 a and b shows the microscopic morphology and element distribution of Ti/Graphite and Ti-Graphite on the side view, where the graphite uniformly covers the surface of the titanium substrate before and after annealing. The results in Fig. S1 show that the graphite layer changes at the interface, where the graphite layer on the Ti/Graphite surface is thicker and unevenly distributed. In contrast, the thickness of the graphite coating on the surface of the annealed Ti-graphite decreases, implying a tighter combination with the Ti matrix. Fig. 2c and d compare the microscopic morphology changes of the samples on the top view (surface) before and after annealing. It can be seen that the surface of the rough Ti substrate (Fig. S2) is covered with a large number of micron-sized flakes after the pencil painting. The uneven surface of Ti/Graphite has many defects and voids, making it difficult to form an effective protective layer on the titanium substrate. In comparison, the surface of Ti-Graphite became smoother after annealing, where the coated graphite flakes form a uniform graphite layer.

The stylus slides gently along the measured surface with the stylus, and the movement reflects the surface profile. By coating only half of the graphite on the titanium substrate, the thickness change of the graphite layer can be observed. From the 3D scanning results of the boundary in Fig. 3 a, it can be seen that the average thickness of the directly painted graphite layer is 935.2 nm. After annealing (Fig. 3b), the combination of graphite and titanium substrate is more closely, and the thickness decreases to 667.1 nm. It can be seen that the annealing process is crucial to the combination of Ti and graphite.

To gain more information about the roughness of the sample surface, an atomic force microscope (AFM) was carried out. Fig. 4 a shows a large number of nanometer-scaled spikes on the surface of Ti/Graphite before annealing. Because the graphite in the pencil has a slideable layered structure, it will generate much debris during the drawing process. Fig. 4b exhibits that the surface of Ti-Graphite is smoother. Therefore, sintering and cooling treatment can eliminate the graphite burrs on the Ti substrate, significantly reducing roughness.

In Raman spectroscopy, the characteristic peaks around 1300 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 belong to D and G peaks of carbon, respectively. The D peak represents defects in the lattice of carbon, while the G peak originates from the in-plane stretching vibrations of the sp
2 hybridization of carbon. The ratio of D- and G-peak intensity (ID/IG) describes the content of defects and graphitization in carbon. Fig. 5 a reveals that the ID/IG value of Ti/Graphite and Ti-Graphite drops from 0.64 to 0.45, indicating that the carbon layer on the surface of Ti-Graphite has fewer defects and possesses more graphitized crystal structure after annealing treatment. The graphite with fewer defects can form a more stable protective layer on the Ti substrate's surface to defend against water and oxygen corrosion.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted to reveal the crystal change of the graphite-coated Ti substrate before and after annealing treatment. Fig. 5b demonstrates that the pure Ti substrate is composed of two crystal structures of α-Ti (ICDD 98-009-9778, Hexagonal) and β-Ti (ICDD 98-009-9778, Cubic). After pencil-drawn on the Ti substrate, a diffraction peak at 27° belongs to the (004) plane of graphite (JCPDS 00-026-1080). It is worth noting that the graphite peak maintains, and two new peaks at 37° and 42° appear and correspond to C1Ti1 (ICDD 98-018-0598, Cubic), indicating the existence of the Ti–C bond in the Ti-Graphite. The annealing treatment reduces the defects in the graphite layer and generates C1Ti1 between the titanium substrate and the graphite layer to further enhance their interfacial interactions.

To evaluate the practical application of Ti-Graphite as a substrate for in-situ growth of electroactive materials, Na3V2(PO4)3 (NVP) was coated on the Ti-Graphite surface by sol-gel and annealing method to test its electrochemical performance as cathode for an aqueous sodium-ion battery. Fig. S4 exhibits that CV curves of NVP on the Ti-Graphite (NVP/Ti-Graphite) have an obvious pair of redox peaks at potentials of 0.4/0.5 V, proving the NVP/Ti-Graphite cathode with electrochemical stability and reversibility allows cations to intercalate/deintercalate in the active material. In contrast, CV curves of NVP on the Ti show little redox peaks, suggesting NVP could not form on the pure Ti substrate. Therefore, the protective graphite layer in Ti-Graphite perseveres titanium's excellent chemical stability and electrical conductivity after high-temperature annealing, which is suitable as the substrate for energy storage devices.

To disclose the evolution mechanism of Ti-Graphite substrate from pencil drawing to high-temperature annealing, molecular dynamics simulations were carried out from an atomic perspective and visually displayed the evolution of microscopic clusters [41]. Fig. 6 shows the schematic model of the pencil-drawing process. To ensure the accuracy of the carbon-coating simulation, the Ti substrate is divided into three regions: boundary, thermostatic, and newtonian layer. The simulation condition was set similarly to the practical nanofabrication, preventing the workpiece from moving and simulating the heat dissipation process. In addition, the periodic boundary is used in the Y-axis direction to reduce the effect of dimensional effects on the simulation results. Although periodic boundary conditions in the X-axis direction are more beneficial to eliminate the size effect, a non-periodic boundary condition in the X-axis direction was set to avoid the overall sliding of the Ti substrate during the carbon-coating process, and fixed boundary conditions were used in the X- and Z-axis directions. To satisfy the above requirements, the atoms in the boundary layer were fixed, and the temperature of the thermostatic layer was maintained at 293 K. The carbon-coating direction is shown in Fig. 6.

Fig. 7 shows the relaxed moments of carbon-coated titanium during the graphite-coating, sintering, and cooling processes. Fig. 7a displays many voids between the carbon layer and Ti substrate, and the fusion of the interface is poor before Sintering, in great agreement with FESEM observation (Fig. 2). The voids will cause poor contact of the carbon layer with the Ti substrate, leading to an unstable interface between the carbon layer and Ti substrate during high-temperature synthesis.

After the graphite-coated Ti substrate was rapidly heated up and then relaxed for 1000 ps at 1026 K, Fig. 7b exhibits that the interface between the carbon layer and the Ti substrate no longer produces voids and has a tighter contact. The mixed layer and fusion of carbon and titanium suggest the formation of a carbon-titanium bond after high-temperature sintering, consistent with XRD analysis (Fig. 5b).

The sintered model was quickly cooled down to 293 K with a cooling rate of 1011 K/s. Fig. 7c reveals that the model has fewer defects in the carbon layer, better integration at the interface, and a flatter surface after cooling down. It is a superior substrate for subsequent chemical synthesis.

Fig. 8 shows the atomic height in the z-axis direction with different colors, where the surface is flatter and lower in height after cooling down. The smoothness of the substrate surface has a vital connection with its wear resistance and oxidation resistance. To discuss the smoothness quantitatively, the root-mean-square roughness is introduced and calculated by the following equation [42].
(1)
R q
a v g
=
∑
i = 1
N
(
Z i
−
Z ¯
)
2
N
where
Z i
denotes the height of the atom
i
in the substrate,
Z ¯
is the height of the average atom in the substrate, and
N
is the total number of atoms in the substrate.

Table 1 exhibits the root-mean-square (RMS) roughness of the simulated model in different processes related to Fig. 8. It can be seen that the RMS values of the model decrease from 13.166 to 8.020 after cooling, implying the surface becomes smoother.

Stress at the interface during heat treatment is common and has been studied in many works [43,44]. Fig. 9 shows the stress distribution of the model during the annealing processes. Because 1026 K has yet to reach Ti's melting point, the Ti substrate's stress value is low. The carbon-titanium mixed layer exhibits high stress when the model was sintered at 1026 K. At high temperatures, the thermal motion between the atoms is more intense, and the carbon atoms in the graphite interact with the Ti atoms on the substrate surface, resulting in higher stresses. In comparison, when it cooled down to room temperature (293 K), there is a little stress at the interface between the carbon layer and Ti substrate. The stress in the carbon layer disappears, where the rearrangement of carbon atoms and Ti–C bonding eliminates the repulsion. Some defects appear in the Ti substrate, which plugs up the stress.

Fig. 10 shows that the average atomic energy changes with temperatures for the carbon and titanium atoms. As the temperature decreases, the average atomic energy of both types of atoms also decreases and varies with different trends. The average atomic energy of carbon atoms decreases rapidly with temperature in the high-temperature range. In contrast, the average atomic energy of Ti decreases slowly at high temperatures and becomes more rapidly at lower temperatures. All atoms tend to be at lower energies, making the model more stable. Comparatively, carbon atoms have lower energies.

Fig. 11 displays the distribution of defects after the model was cooled to 293 K. The illustrations on the right side show the structure type of the atoms and the main types of dislocation lines, which are marked by different colors [45]. To understand the high-temperature protection of the carbon coating on the Ti substrate, only the defective atoms and the carbon atoms were left. There are more linear defects (a-b) on the model's right side. In contrast, there is no defect on the left side, illustrating that carbon coating can suppress the defects well and improve the high-temperature resistance of Ti substrate. The morphology of linear defects is mainly composed of lines and dislocation loops. The defects (b) shows a “knotted” dislocation consisting of multiple dislocation loops connected. The Berger vectors of each dislocation line are marked, which can be better analyzed for dislocations.

In summary, the effect of graphite coating on the Ti substrate and subsequent annealing process was investigated by experimental and simulation methods. In the experiments, a pencil was uniformly drawn on the surface of Ti substrates, which was annealed at 1026 K to obtain graphite-coated Ti substrate (Ti-Graphite) with a stable interface. The surface of pencil-drawn Ti substrate before annealing is constructed by many micron-sized flakes, where the rough surface has many defects and voids and does not form an effective protective layer on the titanium substrate. After annealing treatment, the surface of the graphite-coated Ti substrate becomes smoother and the carbon atoms bond with titanium atoms at the interface, generating a more stable Ti-Graphite substrate with fewer defects. To gain a deep understanding of the synthetic evolution of Ti-Graphite, molecular dynamics simulations were carried out to obtain the atomic structure under carbon-coating, sintering, and cooling down processes. In view of defect distribution, the graphite coating can effectively suppress the generation of linear defects in the Ti substrate, while the defect locations are more prone to plugging stresses. Thus, the graphite coating on the surface Ti substrate can form an effective protective layer to enhance the high-temperature resistance of the Ti substrate, providing a stable substrate to fabricate the all-solid-state batteries.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: No conflict of interest exists in the submission of this manuscript, and the manuscript is approved by all authors for publication. I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously and is not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Guizhou Province Science and Technology Fund (Grant no. ZK[2021] 051, and [2017] 5788), the Industry and Education Combination Innovation Platform of Intelligent Manufacturing and Graduate Joint Training Base at Guizhou University (Grant No: 2020-520000-83-01-324061), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos.52262021, and 51761004), the Scientific and Technological Plan Project of Guizhou Province (Grant No. [2021]060), and the Guizhou Province Graduate Research Fund YJSCXJH[2020]037.
